title: Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search
short_definition: A family of algorithms that find vectors close to a query vector quickly by trading a small amount of accuracy for huge speedups — the foundation of every vector database.
full_explanation:
Computing exact nearest neighbors over a million 1024-dimensional vectors takes a billion floating-point operations per query — too slow for serving. Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms cut that to thousands of operations by intelligently skipping vectors that can't possibly be the answer. The tradeoff: you might miss the true nearest neighbor 1-5% of the time and instead return the second or third closest. For semantic search, that's almost never noticeable.

Three dominant approaches: graph-based (HNSW — navigate a small-world graph by hopping toward similar nodes), tree-based (Annoy, IVF — partition the space and search only relevant partitions), and hash-based (LSH — use locality-sensitive hashing to bucket similar vectors). HNSW dominates production for sub-100ms latency at scale; IVF + Product Quantization dominates when memory matters more than latency.

Every vector database under the hood is essentially an ANN index plus metadata filtering plus some operational glue. Pinecone uses HNSW. Weaviate uses HNSW. Qdrant uses HNSW. Faiss supports many. Milvus supports many. The recall/latency tradeoff is the dial that defines vector-database performance characteristics.
why_it_matters: If you're building anything with semantic search — RAG, recommendation, deduplication, classification by similarity — you're using ANN search whether you know it or not. The recall/latency tradeoff is the single most important hyperparameter you control. Cranking ANN parameters for higher recall slows queries linearly; settling for 95% recall gives you 10x speedups. Knowing what knob you're turning when you tune your vector DB is the difference between informed engineering and cargo-culting Pinecone defaults.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine searching for a book in a library. Exact nearest-neighbor is checking every book on every shelf — accurate, slow. ANN is using the Dewey Decimal system to skip 99% of shelves immediately, then scanning a small section. You might miss a misshelved book occasionally, but you'll be done in 30 seconds instead of 30 hours. The choice of indexing scheme is which classification system you use — different ones suit different libraries.
see_it_in_practice: Faiss benchmarks: a million 768-dim vectors, exact search takes ~5 seconds per query; HNSW with default params takes ~5ms with 95% recall@10. The 1000x speedup for 5% recall loss is the canonical ANN deal. Pick HNSW unless you have a specific reason not to.

related_terms_explained.connection: HNSW is the most-used ANN algorithm in production vector databases.
IVF is the second-most-used ANN approach, especially when paired with Product Quantization.
Faiss is the de facto open-source ANN library implementing all major algorithms.
Vector databases are essentially ANN indexes plus metadata filtering and operational tooling.
ANN search operates on the dense vector embeddings produced by embedding models.

title: Bi-Encoder
short_definition: A retrieval architecture where queries and documents are encoded independently into vectors — fast at scale because document vectors can be precomputed and reused.
full_explanation:
Bi-encoder retrieval splits the work in two: one encoder turns documents into vectors (done once, ahead of time), another encoder turns the query into a vector (done at query time). Similarity is then a single dot product or cosine between the query vector and each document vector. The key win: you precompute every document's vector and store it in a vector index. At query time you encode the query (one forward pass) and do millions of cheap dot products.

Most embedding models (sentence-transformers, OpenAI text-embedding-3, Cohere embed-v3, BGE, E5) are bi-encoders. Many use the same encoder for both queries and documents; some use slightly different prefixes ('query:' vs 'passage:') to specialize. The architecture defines the entire 'first-stage retrieval' layer of modern search systems.

The tradeoff vs cross-encoders: bi-encoders are fast but less accurate. They can't see the query and document together, so they miss subtle semantic interactions. The standard production pattern is bi-encoder for top-100 candidate retrieval, then a cross-encoder reranker for the top-10. Bi-encoder for breadth, cross-encoder for depth.
why_it_matters: Bi-encoders are what makes semantic search work at production scale. The precompute-once-then-dot-product workflow is what enables sub-100ms semantic search across millions of documents. For RAG systems, recommendation systems, and any 'find similar' feature, the bi-encoder is the workhorse. Knowing the architecture explains why your retrieval is fast (precomputed docs) but sometimes shallow (no joint query-doc reasoning) — and why pairing with a reranker is so common.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of bi-encoders like a librarian who's already cataloged every book by topic before you arrive. When you walk in with a question, they consult the catalog and pull matching books in seconds. Compare to a librarian who reads each book cover-to-cover after you ask — that's a cross-encoder. The cataloged approach scales; the read-everything approach is more accurate but only works on small libraries.
see_it_in_practice: sentence-transformers in 5 lines: `from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer; m = SentenceTransformer('BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5'); doc_vecs = m.encode(documents); q_vec = m.encode(query); scores = doc_vecs @ q_vec`. Add Faiss for sub-second retrieval over millions of docs.

related_terms_explained.connection: The accuracy-first alternative to bi-encoders, typically used as a reranker after bi-encoder retrieval.
Bi-encoders are the architecture that produces document and query embeddings.
Bi-encoders are the standard architecture for dense retrieval.
Bi-encoder retrieval is typically followed by cross-encoder reranking for production quality.

code_example:
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
model = SentenceTransformer('BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5')
doc_vecs = model.encode(documents)
query_vec = model.encode(query)
scores = util.cos_sim(query_vec, doc_vecs)

title: BM25
short_definition: A 1990s lexical scoring algorithm that ranks documents by query-term frequency adjusted for document length and term rarity — still the strongest baseline in modern retrieval.
full_explanation:
BM25 (Best Match 25) is a probabilistic retrieval function from the 1980s-90s information retrieval era. It scores each document against a query as a sum, over query terms, of: (term frequency in doc, normalized for doc length) × (inverse document frequency, penalizing common words). The math has two tunable hyperparameters (k1 ≈ 1.2 controlling tf saturation, b ≈ 0.75 controlling length normalization) that turned out to work well for almost every English corpus.

Why it persists in 2026: BM25 is fast, requires no training, no embeddings, no GPU — just an inverted index and the formula. For lexical matches (query and document share exact terms), it's hard to beat. Modern hybrid search routinely combines BM25 with dense retrieval because each catches different things: BM25 nails exact term matches, dense retrieval catches paraphrases. Neither alone matches the combination.

Used by: Lucene/Elasticsearch (default scoring), Whoosh, Tantivy, every classical search engine. Modern vector DBs increasingly bundle BM25 alongside dense retrieval for hybrid mode. Despite a decade of dense-retrieval excitement, BM25 still beats many neural retrievers on out-of-domain test sets — a reminder that classical IR has more legs than neural-first hype suggested.
why_it_matters: If you're building search and skipping BM25 in favor of pure dense retrieval, you're leaving quality on the table. Hybrid (BM25 + dense + reranker) consistently outperforms either alone on real-world benchmarks. BM25 is essentially free — Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, and Tantivy all give you production-grade BM25 in minutes. For RAG systems specifically, hybrid retrieval is the most reliable single-knob upgrade over pure semantic search.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of BM25 as the search algorithm Google used in 2001 — boring, mechanical, but extremely effective at 'find documents containing my words, weighted by how informative those words are.' Dense retrieval handles the cases where the documents talk about your query without using your words. BM25 handles the cases where they share exact vocabulary. Most real queries benefit from both.
see_it_in_practice: In Python: `from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi; bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized_docs); scores = bm25.get_scores(query.split())`. For production: Elasticsearch's default scorer is BM25, requires no setup. Combine with cosine-similarity dense scores via reciprocal rank fusion for hybrid retrieval.

related_terms_explained.connection: BM25 is the canonical sparse-retrieval algorithm based on lexical term matching.
BM25 is one half of the standard hybrid-search recipe (paired with dense retrieval).
BM25 is essentially TF-IDF with better term-frequency saturation and length normalization.
RRF is the standard way to combine BM25 scores with dense retrieval scores.

code_example:
from rank_bm25 import BM25Okapi
tokenized_docs = [doc.split() for doc in corpus]
bm25 = BM25Okapi(tokenized_docs)
scores = bm25.get_scores('what is RAG'.split())

title: Chunking (Document Chunking)
short_definition: Splitting long documents into smaller pieces before indexing — the most underrated knob in RAG quality.
full_explanation:
Embedding models have a max input length (often 512 tokens, sometimes 8K). Documents are usually longer. So you chunk: split each document into pieces small enough to embed, big enough to contain meaningful information. Each chunk gets its own vector and lives independently in the index. Retrieval returns chunks; the LLM sees chunks; quality depends on chunks.

Chunking strategies, from worst to best:
- **Fixed-size character chunks**: split every N characters. Fast, hits sentence boundaries randomly, often produces mid-thought fragments.
- **Fixed-size token chunks with overlap**: same idea but in tokens with 10-20% overlap to reduce edge-loss. The default in LangChain.
- **Sentence-aware chunking**: split on sentence boundaries, group sentences up to a token limit. Better but ignores semantic structure.
- **Recursive structure-aware**: split on markdown headers / HTML / code blocks first, then on paragraphs, then on sentences. Preserves logical units.
- **Semantic chunking**: split based on embedding similarity between adjacent sentences, breaking when semantic distance crosses a threshold. Best quality, slowest.

Chunk size is its own decision: 256 tokens (precision) vs 1024 tokens (context). Smaller chunks retrieve more precisely but lose surrounding context; bigger chunks include more context but the embedding averages too much information. 512 tokens with 50-token overlap is a defensible default for most prose; code and structured data want different strategies.
why_it_matters: If your RAG system retrieves the right document but the chunk-level retrieval pulls a fragment without enough context to answer the question, that's a chunking failure. It's the most common source of 'retrieval looks fine but the LLM still can't answer' bugs. Investing 30 minutes in better chunking strategy (recursive structure-aware, parent-document retrieval) often produces bigger quality gains than upgrading the embedding model. Underrated lever, almost always worth tuning.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of chunking like cutting a book into note cards. If you cut at random page numbers, you'll get card with mid-paragraph fragments — useless. If you cut at chapter boundaries, the cards are too big to flip through quickly. The sweet spot: cut at section boundaries with a few sentences of overlap, so each card is self-contained and adjacent cards share context. The goal is cards that make sense standalone *and* preserve the connections to nearby cards.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain's `RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=50)` is a strong default for prose. For code, use language-specific splitters (Python AST, Markdown header-aware). For mixed content, look at LlamaIndex's `SentenceSplitter` or semantic chunkers like `SemanticChunker`.

related_terms_explained.connection: Chunking is the first preprocessing step in any RAG pipeline.
Parent-document retrieval pairs small chunks for retrieval with bigger parent docs for context.
Chunks are the unit being embedded for vector retrieval.
Sentence-window is a chunking strategy that retrieves sentences with surrounding context.

code_example:
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=50,
    separators=['\n\n', '\n', '. ', ' ', ''],
)
chunks = splitter.split_text(document)

title: ColBERT (Late Interaction Retrieval)
short_definition: A retrieval architecture that stores token-level embeddings instead of one-vector-per-document, scoring with per-token interactions for higher accuracy than bi-encoders.
full_explanation:
Bi-encoders compress a document into a single vector. That works for many cases but loses information — fine semantic distinctions between document parts get averaged away. ColBERT (Khattab & Zaharia, 2020) takes a middle path: encode every token of the document and every token of the query as separate vectors, then score by 'late interaction' — for each query token, find its most-similar document token and sum the similarities.

The math: each document of N tokens stores N vectors (~16 to 128 dimensions per token after compression). The query of M tokens encodes M vectors. Score = sum over query tokens i of max over document tokens j of cos(q_i, d_j). This 'MaxSim' operation captures fine-grained matches — if your query has the word 'enzyme' and the document mentions 'enzyme' specifically in one sentence, that match scores high regardless of the rest of the document content.

The cost: storage is N times bigger than bi-encoder (one vector per token instead of per document). ColBERTv2 (2021) addressed this with quantization, getting storage down to ~20 bytes per token. Production deployments use it in domains where retrieval accuracy matters more than storage cost — legal search, medical search, scientific literature. RAGatouille is the community library that made ColBERT accessible to RAG builders.
why_it_matters: For high-accuracy retrieval applications where bi-encoders aren't quite good enough and rerankers are too slow, ColBERT is a strong middle ground. It's 30-100x more storage than bi-encoder but 10-30x faster than running a cross-encoder over candidates. For RAG systems on large knowledge bases where every quality point matters (legal AI, medical Q&A, technical support), ColBERT is worth knowing about. Less relevant for casual RAG over a small wiki where bi-encoder + reranker handles things fine.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of bi-encoder as taking a 5-second blurry photo of each document for matching. ColBERT takes a high-resolution photo of every page. When you query, bi-encoder matches blurry photos to blurry photos (fast, sometimes loses detail). ColBERT matches your query to specific page-level details (slower, finds the actual relevant page even in long documents). The price is storage: a photo album per document instead of a thumbnail.
see_it_in_practice: RAGatouille (github.com/bclavie/RAGatouille) ships ColBERTv2 with one-line indexing and retrieval. `from ragatouille import RAGPretrainedModel; rag = RAGPretrainedModel.from_pretrained('colbert-ir/colbertv2.0'); rag.index(collection=docs); rag.search(query)`. Quality often noticeably better than bi-encoder for technical documents.

related_terms_explained.connection: ColBERT is a higher-accuracy alternative to bi-encoder retrieval.
ColBERT sits between bi-encoder (fast, low accuracy) and cross-encoder (slow, high accuracy).
ColBERT is a specialized form of dense retrieval using token-level embeddings.
SPLADE is the sparse counterpart — both improve on standard dense retrieval differently.

code_example:
from ragatouille import RAGPretrainedModel
rag = RAGPretrainedModel.from_pretrained('colbert-ir/colbertv2.0')
rag.index(collection=documents, index_name='my-corpus')
results = rag.search('your query', k=10)

title: Contextual Compression (Retrieval)
short_definition: Filtering or summarizing retrieved chunks to keep only the parts relevant to the user's query — reduces context size and removes distracting irrelevant text.
full_explanation:
RAG retrievers commonly return chunks with mixed content — only some sentences within the chunk actually answer the question. Sending the whole chunk wastes tokens and confuses the model with irrelevant context. Contextual compression runs a second pass on retrieved chunks to extract only the relevant pieces.

Three common implementations:
1. **LLM-based filtering**: ask a small LLM 'is any of this relevant to: {query}?' for each chunk; drop chunks scored low.
2. **LLM-based extraction**: ask a small LLM to extract only relevant sentences from each chunk into a compressed version.
3. **Embedding-based filtering**: re-embed chunk sentences against the query, keep only sentences above similarity threshold.

LangChain's `ContextualCompressionRetriever` wraps any base retriever with a compressor. LlamaIndex calls similar functionality 'response synthesis' or 'tree summarization'. The cost is one extra LLM/embedding call per retrieved chunk, but the savings — both tokens and quality — usually pay back.

Known failure mode: aggressive compression drops context the LLM actually needs. The typical solution is to compress for token-budget reasons but include 'parent document' references for the LLM to expand if needed.
why_it_matters: If your RAG system is hitting context limits, producing distracted answers, or paying too much per query, contextual compression is one of the easiest wins. A small Haiku-class model running compression cuts token cost on the main model dramatically while typically improving quality (less noise, more signal). For long-document RAG and multi-document agent contexts especially, compression is increasingly standard.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of contextual compression like having a research assistant pre-highlight the relevant parts of each document before handing them to your boss. The boss reads less, makes faster decisions, and is less distracted by irrelevant material. The assistant's reading time is the cost; the boss's time and decision quality are the benefit. For most RAG systems, the boss (your LLM) is the expensive resource, so the trade is favorable.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain: `from langchain.retrievers import ContextualCompressionRetriever; from langchain.retrievers.document_compressors import LLMChainExtractor; comp = LLMChainExtractor.from_llm(haiku); retriever = ContextualCompressionRetriever(base_compressor=comp, base_retriever=base)`. Now retrieve runs compression automatically.

related_terms_explained.connection: Contextual compression is a standard optimization layer in production RAG pipelines.
Compression is sometimes paired with reranking for two-stage filtering.
Compression effectively does dynamic re-chunking based on query relevance.
Compression is one of the main techniques for fitting RAG context within token budgets.

code_example:
from langchain.retrievers import ContextualCompressionRetriever
from langchain.retrievers.document_compressors import LLMChainExtractor

compressor = LLMChainExtractor.from_llm(small_llm)
retriever = ContextualCompressionRetriever(
    base_compressor=compressor,
    base_retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(),
)

title: Cosine Similarity
short_definition: A measure of how similar two vectors are based on the angle between them, ignoring their lengths — the standard distance metric in semantic search.
full_explanation:
Two vectors can be close in different ways. Euclidean distance measures how far apart their tips are. Cosine similarity measures the angle between them — if they point in the same direction it's 1.0, opposite is -1.0, perpendicular is 0. The math: cos(θ) = (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||).

Cosine wins for embedding-based retrieval because embedding magnitudes are typically not informative — what matters is the *direction* in semantic space. A document about cats and another about cats have similar directions; a longer document about cats doesn't have a 'bigger' cat-direction, just more text. Normalizing away magnitude makes comparisons scale-invariant and intuitive.

Two practical points: (1) if your vectors are already normalized to unit length (which most embedding models do by default), cosine similarity reduces to a dot product — which is much faster on GPU. (2) Most ANN libraries (Faiss, hnswlib) let you choose between cosine, dot, and Euclidean — pick cosine or dot product for embeddings, Euclidean only for non-embedding vectors. Modern embedding APIs (OpenAI, Cohere, BGE, E5) all return normalized vectors so cosine = dot product.
why_it_matters: Foundational concept for anyone doing semantic search, recommendation, or embedding-based clustering. Misuse — using Euclidean on un-normalized vectors and getting weird results, or computing cosine inefficiently when dot product would do — wastes compute and produces confusing scores. Five minutes spent understanding cosine similarity grounds the rest of vector-database work in clear math.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine arrows from the origin pointing in different directions. Cosine similarity ignores how long the arrows are and only measures whether they point similar ways. Two arrows pointing east, one short and one long, have cosine 1.0 — they're 'pointing the same way' even though they're different lengths. For text embeddings, direction = topic, length = mostly irrelevant. So cosine = topic similarity.
see_it_in_practice: In numpy: `np.dot(a, b) / (np.linalg.norm(a) * np.linalg.norm(b))`. With normalized vectors: just `a @ b`. In sentence-transformers: `util.cos_sim(query_emb, doc_embs)` returns a tensor of similarities sorted-able for top-k retrieval.

related_terms_explained.connection: Cosine similarity is the standard scoring function for comparing embeddings.
For unit-normalized vectors, cosine similarity equals dot product (faster to compute).
All vector databases support cosine similarity as a primary distance metric.
ANN algorithms approximate the cosine-similarity nearest neighbors in vector spaces.

title: Cross-Encoder
short_definition: A retrieval architecture that takes a query and a document together as one input and outputs a relevance score — much more accurate than bi-encoders but too slow to score millions of documents.
full_explanation:
Cross-encoder retrieval feeds (query, document) pairs into a single model that outputs one relevance score. The model attends jointly across query and document tokens, catching subtle semantic matches that single-vector bi-encoders miss. The result is consistently 5-15% better recall@10 on retrieval benchmarks vs. comparable bi-encoders.

The price is brutal. To rank a million documents against one query, you must run the model a million times — one per (query, doc) pair. At ~50ms per scoring call, that's 14 hours per query. Useless for first-stage retrieval.

The production pattern: use a fast bi-encoder to retrieve top-100 candidates, then a cross-encoder to rerank the top-100 down to the top-10 you actually use. Cross-encoder rerankers add ~500ms-2s per query but typically improve final answer quality dramatically. The most-used open cross-encoders are Cohere's rerank API, BAAI/bge-reranker (open weights), Jina's reranker, and the original cross-encoder/ms-marco models from sentence-transformers.

Why this works: bi-encoder embeds in 'document space' and 'query space' independently — the model has no chance to compare them jointly. Cross-encoder gets full attention over the combined input, so it can detect 'the document mentions X, but query is asking about something X-adjacent that the document also discusses' — fine semantic relationships that bi-encoders flatten.
why_it_matters: If you're building RAG and not using a reranker, you're missing the single biggest accuracy improvement available without changing models. Adding a cross-encoder reranker after bi-encoder retrieval reliably improves end-to-end answer quality. Cohere Rerank, BGE Reranker, Jina Reranker — all easy drop-ins. The latency cost (~500ms) is usually acceptable; the quality gain is usually substantial.
how_to_think_about_it: Bi-encoder is a librarian who pre-categorized every book. Cross-encoder is a librarian who reads each candidate book carefully when you ask a question. The pre-categorizer is fast but coarse; the careful reader is slow but precise. Production search hires both: the categorizer narrows millions to dozens, the careful reader picks the best of the dozens. You'd never hire just the slow one (too slow) or just the fast one (too coarse).
see_it_in_practice: BGE Reranker: `from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder; reranker = CrossEncoder('BAAI/bge-reranker-large'); scores = reranker.predict([(query, doc) for doc in candidates]); top_docs = sorted(zip(candidates, scores), key=lambda x: -x[1])[:10]`. Cohere's rerank API is even simpler if you don't mind the third-party call.

related_terms_explained.connection: Bi-encoders feed the candidates that cross-encoders rerank.
Cross-encoder is the most common reranker architecture.
Cross-encoder reranking is one of the most reliable RAG quality improvements.
ColBERT is a middle-ground architecture between bi-encoder speed and cross-encoder accuracy.

code_example:
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder
reranker = CrossEncoder('BAAI/bge-reranker-large')
pairs = [(query, doc) for doc in candidates]
scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
reranked = [c for _, c in sorted(zip(scores, candidates), reverse=True)]

title: Dense Retrieval
short_definition: Retrieval based on learned dense vector embeddings (continuous, low-dimensional) — captures semantic similarity that lexical methods like BM25 miss.
full_explanation:
Classical retrieval (BM25, TF-IDF) works on word matching: documents containing query words score high. This breaks when the query and document use different vocabulary for the same idea ('automobile' vs 'car'). Dense retrieval addresses this by encoding both queries and documents as dense vectors in a shared semantic space, then ranking by vector similarity. The vector for 'automobile' and 'car' end up near each other in that space — making the match work.

Mechanically, dense retrieval = bi-encoder embedding + ANN index for fast similarity search. The bi-encoder is trained on retrieval-style supervision (query-document positive/negative pairs) so the embedding space is shaped specifically for retrieval — not just general semantic similarity. Models like BGE, E5, GTE, OpenAI text-embedding-3, and Cohere embed-v3 are all dense retrievers in this sense.

Weaknesses: dense retrieval struggles with rare terms (the embedding loses the signal of an unusual word in a sea of common ones), exact matches (a person's name might not embed near itself if it wasn't in training), and out-of-domain queries (BGE trained on web text degrades on medical literature). Hybrid (dense + sparse) consistently beats either alone for these reasons. Dense retrieval is necessary, not sufficient.
why_it_matters: Dense retrieval is what makes 'semantic search' a thing. Without it, RAG is just keyword matching, which fails the moment a user phrases something differently than the documents do. For practitioners building any retrieval system, dense retrieval (with a good open-source embedding model) is the baseline starting point. The follow-up question is always 'should I add BM25 for hybrid?' — answer: yes, almost always.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine arranging documents on a giant map where similar topics sit near each other — sports here, cooking there, finance over yonder. Dense retrieval embeds your query as a coordinate on that map and returns the documents nearest to it. Compared to BM25, which is 'find documents containing my words,' dense retrieval is 'find documents about topics near my topic.' Different superpower; complementary to lexical search.
see_it_in_practice: BGE in 5 lines via sentence-transformers, vectors stored in Faiss or any vector DB, cosine similarity for ranking. The whole stack — embedding model + vector index + retrieval — is open-source and runnable on CPU for small corpora, GPU at scale.

related_terms_explained.connection: Bi-encoder is the standard architecture used to produce dense retrieval embeddings.
Dense retrieval is fundamentally an embedding-similarity search.
Lexical alternatives like BM25; hybrid combines both for best results.
Combining dense and sparse retrieval; the typical production approach.
Dense retrieval is the primary use case for vector databases.

title: FAISS
short_definition: Facebook AI's open-source library for efficient similarity search and clustering of dense vectors — the underlying engine behind many vector databases.
full_explanation:
FAISS (Facebook AI Similarity Search) ships every major ANN algorithm — flat (exact), IVF, HNSW, PQ, IVF-PQ, OPQ — under one Python+C++ interface. Open-source since 2017 and battle-tested at Meta scale. Most production vector setups either use FAISS directly (you build your own index management) or use a vector database that wraps FAISS internally.

The library is unusually flexible. You can switch between exact and approximate search by changing one line. You can quantize vectors to 8 or 4 bits to save memory. You can run on CPU or GPU. You can shard across multiple GPUs. The downside is the API has a learning curve — index types are spelled like 'IVF1024,PQ32' and parameter tuning is its own art. The official docs and tutorials cover the basics; the wiki covers the gotchas.

Who uses it raw vs wrapped: ML researchers, embedding-generation pipelines, and anyone with custom retrieval workflows often use FAISS directly. Application developers tend to use vector DBs (Weaviate, Qdrant, Pinecone) that abstract FAISS and add metadata filtering, RBAC, multi-tenancy. For learning ANN concepts hands-on, FAISS is unmatched — you can implement HNSW, IVF, and PQ as one-liners and benchmark them against each other in 20 minutes.
why_it_matters: FAISS is the foundational library behind most semantic search infrastructure. Even when you're using a managed vector DB, knowing how FAISS indices work helps you reason about your provider's tradeoffs. For research pipelines, embedding-generation workflows, and anywhere you need fine-grained control over the index, FAISS is the right tool. For applications, the higher-level vector databases are more convenient but FAISS literacy pays off.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of FAISS as the engine and vector DBs as the cars. Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant — they all need an engine to do the actual nearest-neighbor search; many use FAISS or libraries derived from it. Driving the engine directly (FAISS) gives you fine control but requires more knowledge; driving the car (vector DB) is more convenient but the engine is hidden. Both are valid; pick based on whether you need the customization.
see_it_in_practice: Indexing in 5 lines: `import faiss; index = faiss.IndexFlatL2(dim); index.add(vectors); D, I = index.search(query_vec, k=10)`. For 1M+ vectors switch to IVF: `faiss.index_factory(dim, 'IVF1024,Flat')` for a balanced speed/accuracy index.

related_terms_explained.connection: FAISS implements all the major ANN algorithms.
FAISS supports HNSW as one of its index types.
FAISS's IVF implementation is widely used in production.
FAISS pioneered Product Quantization in production ANN.
Many vector databases use FAISS as their underlying ANN engine.

code_example:
import faiss, numpy as np
index = faiss.IndexHNSWFlat(768, 32)  # 32 = M parameter
index.add(np.array(doc_vectors).astype('float32'))
D, I = index.search(np.array([query_vector]).astype('float32'), k=10)

title: Graph RAG
short_definition: A RAG variant that builds a knowledge graph from your documents during indexing and retrieves connected subgraphs at query time — captures relationships that vector search misses.
full_explanation:
Standard vector RAG retrieves chunks based on semantic similarity to the query. That works for 'lookup' questions but breaks on 'connection' questions: 'What companies did this person found?' requires linking person→company entities, not finding semantically similar text. Graph RAG addresses this by extracting entities and relationships from documents during indexing, building them into a knowledge graph, and retrieving subgraphs at query time.

Mechanically, indexing has an extra LLM-driven step: extract triples (subject, relation, object) from each chunk, deduplicate entities, build the graph in a database (Neo4j, NebulaGraph, Memgraph, or Microsoft's GraphRAG with custom storage). At retrieval time, the query is parsed for entities, the relevant subgraph is extracted, and both raw text and graph context are sent to the LLM.

Microsoft's GraphRAG (released mid-2024) popularized the pattern with a published implementation that uses LLM-based community detection to summarize related concepts at multiple abstraction levels. Cost is significant — graph construction can use 10-100x more LLM calls than standard chunking — but for use cases involving entity relationships, multi-hop reasoning, or unstructured-to-structured analysis, the quality bump is substantial.
why_it_matters: If your RAG application needs to answer 'connection' questions ('how is X related to Y?', 'what are all the things in category Z?', 'who works with whom?'), pure vector RAG hits a quality ceiling. Graph RAG breaks through it. The cost is real (more compute at indexing, more complexity at query) but for the right use cases — investigative analytics, scientific literature reviews, organizational intelligence — it's the right architectural choice. For 80% of standard RAG, vector retrieval remains simpler and good enough.
how_to_think_about_it: Vector RAG is like having a search engine for your documents. Graph RAG is like having a Wikipedia infobox for your documents — entities and relationships pre-extracted, navigable by clicking links. For 'tell me about X' questions, both work. For 'show me everyone connected to X who also works on Y,' you need the graph.
see_it_in_practice: Microsoft's GraphRAG: github.com/microsoft/graphrag. LlamaIndex's PropertyGraphIndex offers similar functionality with more flexibility. Note: indexing 1000 docs into a graph can take hours and cost $50-200 in LLM calls; budget appropriately and start with a small corpus to validate.

related_terms_explained.connection: Graph RAG is an architectural variant of standard RAG focused on entity relationships.
The underlying data structure that powers Graph RAG retrieval.
Graph RAG indexing requires LLM-based entity and relationship extraction.
Standard RAG alternative; Graph RAG often uses graph DB instead of or alongside vector DB.

title: HNSW (Hierarchical Navigable Small World)
short_definition: A graph-based approximate nearest neighbor algorithm that organizes vectors in a multi-layer skip-list-like structure — the dominant ANN choice for production vector search.
full_explanation:
HNSW (Malkov & Yashunin, 2016) builds a layered graph of vectors. The bottom layer connects every vector to its closest neighbors. Higher layers contain progressively fewer vectors, each connected to others at the same layer plus down-pointers to the layer below. Search starts at the top: find the entry point closest to the query, descend one layer, find the closest at the new layer, descend again, repeating until you hit the bottom and converge on the nearest neighbors.

The magic: this two-step navigation finds approximate nearest neighbors in O(log N) time instead of O(N) for exact search. With careful parameter tuning (M = neighbors per node, ef_construction = build-time exploration, ef_search = query-time exploration), HNSW achieves 95-99% recall at sub-millisecond latency for most production-scale corpora.

Used by virtually every modern vector database: Pinecone, Weaviate, Qdrant, Milvus all default to HNSW. FAISS supports it. hnswlib is the reference C++ implementation with Python bindings. The downside is memory cost — HNSW typically uses 1.5-2x the raw vector size for graph overhead, which matters at billion-scale. IVF + Product Quantization is the alternative when memory matters more than latency.
why_it_matters: HNSW is the algorithm under the hood of nearly every vector search system you'll touch. Knowing how it works explains why your vector DB has these specific knobs (M, ef), why higher recall costs more latency, and why memory overhead is what it is. For tuning vector retrieval performance, HNSW literacy is the foundation. Most defaults are sensible, but the right tuning can give you 5x speedups or 5% recall gains depending on what you optimize for.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine the vectors as cities on a map. HNSW pre-builds three road networks: highways connecting major cities (top layer), regional roads (middle layer), local streets (bottom layer). To find the city nearest to your destination, you start on the highway (covers ground fast), exit to a regional road when you're close, then follow local streets to home. The hierarchy lets you cover distance fast, then refine. Without it, you'd be navigating the entire local-street network from the start — far too slow.
see_it_in_practice: hnswlib in 5 lines: `import hnswlib; index = hnswlib.Index(space='cosine', dim=768); index.init_index(max_elements=1000000, ef_construction=200, M=16); index.add_items(vectors); index.set_ef(50); labels, distances = index.knn_query(query, k=10)`. Tweak M (16-64) and ef (50-500) to trade recall for speed.

related_terms_explained.connection: HNSW is the most popular ANN algorithm in production vector search.
FAISS supports HNSW as one of its index types.
IVF is the alternative ANN approach when memory matters more than latency.
Most vector databases use HNSW as their default index.

code_example:
import hnswlib
index = hnswlib.Index(space='cosine', dim=768)
index.init_index(max_elements=1_000_000, ef_construction=200, M=16)
index.add_items(vectors, ids)
index.set_ef(50)
labels, distances = index.knn_query(query_vec, k=10)

title: Hybrid Search
short_definition: Combining sparse (BM25) and dense (vector) retrieval and merging the results — consistently better than either alone.
full_explanation:
Sparse retrieval (BM25) handles exact term matches, rare words, and proper nouns well; dense retrieval handles paraphrases, semantic synonyms, and conceptual similarity well. Each catches what the other misses. Hybrid search runs both, then merges the result sets into a unified ranked list.

The merge step is where strategy matters. Three common approaches:
1. **Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)**: each candidate's final score = sum over methods of 1/(rank_in_method + k). Robust, no score normalization needed, easy to tune. Most-used in production.
2. **Convex combination**: final_score = α × dense_score + (1 − α) × sparse_score. Requires score normalization (different scales), tune α as a hyperparameter.
3. **Reranking**: use both methods to retrieve top-K each, union them, then send everything to a cross-encoder reranker.

Support: Elasticsearch's hybrid mode (built-in BM25 + vectors), Weaviate (BM25 + dense, configurable fusion), Qdrant (BM25 + dense), Pinecone (recently added). Most production setups also bolt a cross-encoder reranker on top of hybrid retrieval — the three-stage 'BM25 + dense → RRF merge → cross-encoder rerank' pipeline is the high-quality default.
why_it_matters: If you're running pure dense retrieval, you're missing recall on queries that contain rare terms, exact phrases, or proper nouns. Adding BM25 + RRF on top is usually a 10-20% recall improvement with negligible latency cost. For RAG systems where retrieval quality directly impacts answer quality, hybrid is essentially the modern default. Most vector DBs make this trivial to configure.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of hybrid search like a job interview with two interviewers. One asks pattern-matching questions (BM25: 'have you used X technology?' — good at exact matches). The other asks open-ended questions (dense: 'tell me about a time you solved a hard problem' — good at gestalt assessment). Each catches different signals. The hiring decision combines both perspectives. Same with retrieval: each method catches what the other misses; combining produces better candidates than either alone.
see_it_in_practice: Weaviate hybrid: `client.query.get('Article', ['title']).with_hybrid(query='your query', alpha=0.5).with_limit(10).do()`. Alpha controls dense weight. Or DIY with RRF: retrieve top-50 from each method, merge by rank-reciprocal sum, take top-10.

related_terms_explained.connection: BM25 is the standard sparse retrieval component of hybrid search.
Dense retrieval is the semantic component combined with BM25.
RRF is the most common merge strategy for hybrid retrieval.
Hybrid search is often paired with cross-encoder reranking for further quality gains.

title: Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE)
short_definition: A retrieval technique where the LLM first writes a hypothetical answer to the query, then you embed that answer (not the query) for vector search — improves recall on hard queries.
full_explanation:
HyDE (Gao et al., 2022) addresses an asymmetry problem. Queries are usually short and abstract ('how does HNSW work?'). Documents are usually long and concrete (a paragraph explaining HNSW). The embedding of the query and the embedding of the document live in slightly different distributions — the model never saw queries during training, only document-style text. Retrieval suffers.

HyDE's trick: ask the LLM to generate a hypothetical answer to the query first ('HNSW works by building a layered graph...'). The hypothetical answer is in the same distribution as real documents — same length, same style, same terminology density. Embed THAT, then run vector search. The hypothetical doc acts as a query rewriting in the document distribution.

The results vary. On out-of-domain or technical queries, HyDE can boost recall 10-30%. On in-domain or simple queries, it sometimes hurts — the LLM hallucinates incorrect details that misdirect the embedding. Production usage is mixed; some systems run HyDE selectively (only on queries flagged as 'hard' by a classifier), others always, others never.
why_it_matters: An interesting tool for the RAG toolkit, especially for systems handling expert-level queries where the user's wording diverges from how the documents discuss the topic. The cost is one extra LLM call per query (~500-1500ms). For production RAG applications, worth A/B testing on your real queries — HyDE is one of those 'sometimes magic, sometimes harmful' techniques where empirical testing on your specific corpus matters more than received wisdom.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of HyDE as 'pretend you already know the answer, then search for documents that look like that pretend answer.' Surprisingly often, even a hallucinated answer is in the right semantic neighborhood of real answers. So search using the hallucinated answer as the query lands on real documents that confirm or correct the hallucination. The LLM acts as a translator from 'question style' to 'answer style.'
see_it_in_practice: DIY in 10 lines: prompt LLM to 'generate a paragraph that would answer this question'; embed the paragraph; vector search; pass real retrieved docs to a second LLM call for the actual answer. LangChain has a HypotheticalDocumentEmbedder helper.

related_terms_explained.connection: HyDE is a query-rewriting technique for improving RAG retrieval recall.
HyDE is one specific query-rewriting strategy among many.
HyDE works by embedding hypothetical documents instead of raw queries.
HyDE is a dense-retrieval enhancement; doesn't apply to sparse retrieval.

code_example:
# Simplified HyDE
hyp_doc = llm.invoke(f'Write a paragraph that answers: {query}')
hyp_emb = embedder.embed_query(hyp_doc)
docs = vectorstore.similarity_search_by_vector(hyp_emb, k=5)

title: IVF (Inverted File Index)
short_definition: An ANN algorithm that partitions vectors into clusters during indexing and searches only the clusters nearest to the query — memory-efficient alternative to HNSW.
full_explanation:
IVF (Inverted File) builds an index in two steps. First, it runs k-means clustering on your vectors to find K centroids (typically 1000-10000 clusters). Second, each vector is assigned to its nearest centroid, creating an inverted file: centroid → list of vectors in that cluster. At query time, find the few clusters whose centroids are closest to the query, then exhaustively search just those clusters.

Key hyperparameters: nlist (number of clusters, ~sqrt(N) is a common heuristic) and nprobe (clusters to search at query time, higher = better recall, slower). With 1M vectors, nlist=1000 means each cluster has ~1000 vectors; searching nprobe=10 clusters checks 10K vectors instead of 1M — 100x speedup.

IVF's killer feature is composability with quantization. IVF-PQ stores only quantized 8-byte representations of each vector instead of full 4KB+ embeddings — massive memory savings (50-200x) at modest accuracy cost. For billion-scale corpora where HNSW's memory overhead becomes prohibitive, IVF-PQ is the workhorse. Used by Spotify, YouTube, and many image-search backends.
why_it_matters: For large-scale retrieval (>10M vectors), memory becomes the dominant cost. HNSW's 1.5-2x overhead becomes painful at billion-scale; IVF-PQ at 50-200x compression is the right tool. For application-level retrieval (most RAG, recommendation systems with <10M items), HNSW is simpler and faster. Knowing which is which lets you pick the right algorithm for your scale instead of defaulting to HNSW everywhere.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine organizing a library by first sorting books into rough genre buckets. To find a specific book, you check which bucket it'd be in (one quick decision), then scan that bucket carefully. This is faster than scanning all books — but only as good as your bucket assignment. If you're flexible about checking neighboring buckets too (nprobe=2 or 3), you trade some scan time for better odds of finding the target. IVF formalizes this approach for vectors.
see_it_in_practice: FAISS IVF: `index = faiss.index_factory(768, 'IVF1024,Flat'); index.train(training_vectors); index.add(all_vectors); index.nprobe = 10; D, I = index.search(query, k=10)`. The training step computes centroids; cheap one-time cost. For huge corpora, swap 'Flat' for 'PQ32' to enable quantization.

related_terms_explained.connection: IVF is one of the major ANN algorithm families.
HNSW is the alternative when memory isn't constrained; IVF when it is.
IVF-PQ combines IVF clustering with PQ vector compression for billion-scale search.
FAISS provides the most-used implementation of IVF and IVF-PQ.

code_example:
import faiss
index = faiss.index_factory(768, 'IVF1024,PQ32')
index.train(train_vectors)
index.add(all_vectors)
index.nprobe = 10
D, I = index.search(query, k=10)

title: Knowledge Graph (RAG Context)
short_definition: A structured representation of entities and their relationships as nodes and edges — used in RAG to capture connections that vector embeddings flatten.
full_explanation:
A knowledge graph stores facts as triples: (subject, relationship, object). 'Anthropic founded by Dario Amodei.' 'Claude developed by Anthropic.' 'Dario Amodei previously at OpenAI.' The graph emerges from connecting these triples — Dario links to both Anthropic and OpenAI, Anthropic links to both Dario and Claude, etc.

Traditionally built by hand from databases (Wikidata, DBpedia), curated for years. The 2024-2025 trend is LLM-built knowledge graphs from unstructured documents — feed an LLM each chunk, ask it to extract triples, deduplicate entities, store in a graph DB. Microsoft's GraphRAG popularized this approach with a public implementation. The result is a structured layer on top of your unstructured corpus.

In RAG context, knowledge graphs unlock 'connection' queries that vector retrieval can't: 'Show me all companies founded by ex-Googlers.' 'What papers cite both X and Y?' 'Who owns what subsidiaries?' These multi-hop, relational queries require graph traversal. Vector retrieval can find documents that mention X and Y separately but can't reliably find the connection between them.

Storage: Neo4j (most popular), NebulaGraph, Memgraph, AWS Neptune. Querying typically uses Cypher (Neo4j's query language) or Gremlin. LLM agents can be taught to write Cypher queries against your graph for natural-language access.
why_it_matters: For RAG systems answering questions about complex domains with rich entity relationships — pharma research, organizational analytics, supply chain, financial intelligence — knowledge graphs provide the structural backbone vector retrieval can't. The cost is real (more indexing compute, more complex stack) but the queries you unlock are otherwise impossible. For simpler domains, vector RAG suffices. Knowing when to reach for graph RAG is part of mature RAG architecture decision-making.
how_to_think_about_it: Vector embeddings are like Polaroids of each document — capture overall vibe, lose detail and connections. Knowledge graphs are like a wiring diagram showing how every entity connects to every other entity — explicit, queryable, structured. Some questions are answered by browsing photos (vector). Others by reading the wiring diagram (graph). Many real questions need both, which is why production systems often combine vector + graph indices.
see_it_in_practice: Microsoft GraphRAG: github.com/microsoft/graphrag. Or with LlamaIndex: `PropertyGraphIndex.from_documents(docs, llm=gpt4)` builds the graph automatically; query via `index.as_query_engine().query('your question')`.

related_terms_explained.connection: Graph RAG is the RAG architecture pattern built on knowledge graphs.
Knowledge graphs add structural retrieval capability to standard RAG.
Building knowledge graphs from text requires entity and relationship extraction.
Often complementary; vector DBs handle semantic search, graph DBs handle relationships.

title: Metadata Filtering (Vector DB)
short_definition: Restricting vector search to documents matching specific metadata criteria (date range, author, category) — combines structured filters with semantic search.
full_explanation:
Vector search alone doesn't handle 'find articles about climate change published after 2024 by Reuters.' That requires combining semantic similarity (climate change) with structured filters (date range, source). Metadata filtering attaches structured fields to each indexed vector and applies SQL-like filters before or after similarity search.

Three implementation strategies:
1. **Pre-filter**: apply metadata filter first (build a candidate set), then run ANN search only on those vectors. Simple but breaks ANN index efficiency for selective filters.
2. **Post-filter**: run full ANN search, return top-K, drop those that don't match metadata. Wastes query work; can return fewer results than requested.
3. **Filtered ANN**: integrate filter into the search itself (Qdrant's payload filtering, Milvus's filtered search). Best of both worlds; requires index support.

Qdrant pioneered native filtered ANN with payload-aware HNSW. Pinecone added filterable indexes. Weaviate has where filters. Most modern vector DBs do this well; FAISS-based DIY setups need extra work to support it efficiently.

Real-world filters: tenant_id (multi-tenant SaaS), date range (recency), source (only Wikipedia), category (only support docs), language, region, security clearance. Multi-tenant filtering is the most common production case — every query needs to be scoped to one tenant's documents.
why_it_matters: Almost no production RAG runs without metadata filtering. Multi-tenant SaaS apps need tenant isolation. News apps need date filters. Support apps need product-version filters. For anyone building real applications on top of vector search, choosing a vector DB with first-class metadata filtering (Qdrant, Weaviate, Milvus, Pinecone) saves enormous architectural pain compared to bolt-on solutions.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of vector search as 'find me semantically similar items' and metadata filters as 'but only from this subset.' Without filters, you can't enforce 'only my organization's documents' or 'only items from this product line.' With filters, vector search becomes a two-dimensional retrieval: 'similar AND matching constraints.' Both dimensions are usually needed in real applications.
see_it_in_practice: Qdrant: `client.search(collection_name='my-coll', query_vector=q, query_filter={'must': [{'key':'tenant_id','match':{'value':'acme'}},{'key':'date','range':{'gte':1704067200}}]}, limit=10)`. Filtered HNSW handles selectivity efficiently across the index.

related_terms_explained.connection: Metadata filtering is a core feature distinguishing modern vector DBs from raw ANN libraries.
Production RAG systems almost always use metadata filtering for scope/tenancy.
Naive ANN doesn't support filters; production needs filter-aware ANN implementations.

title: Multi-Query Retrieval
short_definition: Asking an LLM to rephrase a query into 3-5 variants, retrieving for each, and merging results — improves recall by exploring multiple semantic directions.
full_explanation:
Single-query retrieval has a fundamental limitation: one query embedding lands on one point in semantic space. If your real intent has multiple aspects, the embedding flattens them and you retrieve documents matching the central tendency, missing documents matching specific aspects. Multi-query retrieval addresses this by generating multiple query variants — each highlighting a different angle — and running retrieval for each.

Workflow: prompt the LLM 'generate 4 different rephrasings of this query, each emphasizing a different aspect.' Each variant gets embedded, runs vector search, returns top-K. Union the results, deduplicate, optionally rerank, return final top-K. The LLM call is small (Haiku-class works fine) but adds ~500ms latency.

Failure mode: if the LLM-generated variants don't actually highlight different aspects but just paraphrase, you waste compute without recall gains. Better prompts ('generate variants from different perspectives: technical, practical, comparative...') help. RAG-Fusion adds reciprocal rank fusion across the variants for better merging.
why_it_matters: Useful when your queries are short, abstract, or have multiple aspects, and your standard retrieval is missing relevant documents. Particularly valuable for question-answering over technical documentation where the same underlying question can be asked many ways. The cost is one extra LLM call; the recall improvement on hard queries is often 10-20%. Easy A/B test on real traffic.
how_to_think_about_it: Imagine asking a librarian a question. They might bring back the most obvious 5 books matching your wording. A multi-query retriever is like having the librarian ask you to rephrase the question 4 times, then bring 5 books for each phrasing. You get 20 candidates total, with broader coverage of what you might have meant. The downside is the librarian charges for each lookup — multiply your API calls by 4-5 times.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain: `from langchain.retrievers.multi_query import MultiQueryRetriever; retriever = MultiQueryRetriever.from_llm(retriever=base_retriever, llm=llm)`. Drop-in replacement; runs the variant-generation under the hood.

related_terms_explained.connection: Multi-query is one of several recall-enhancement techniques in modern RAG.
RAG-Fusion adds RRF merging on top of multi-query retrieval.
Multi-query is a specific query-rewriting strategy that produces multiple variants.
Different query-side technique; HyDE generates a hypothetical doc, multi-query generates multiple queries.

code_example:
from langchain.retrievers.multi_query import MultiQueryRetriever
retriever = MultiQueryRetriever.from_llm(
    retriever=vectorstore.as_retriever(),
    llm=ChatAnthropic(model='claude-haiku-4-5'),
)

title: Parent-Document Retriever
short_definition: A RAG pattern where small chunks are used for retrieval (precision) but their larger parent documents are returned to the LLM (context) — gets the best of both chunk sizes.
full_explanation:
Standard RAG forces a chunk-size tradeoff: small chunks retrieve precisely but lack context; large chunks have context but retrieve fuzzily. Parent-document retrieval breaks the tradeoff. During indexing, you embed and index small chunks (e.g., 256 tokens), but each chunk has a pointer to its larger parent (e.g., 2000-token section). At retrieval, you find candidates by small-chunk similarity, then return the parent document(s) to the LLM.

The small chunk acts as a needle. The parent document provides context around the needle. The LLM sees the full context but you searched on the precise embedding. Often dramatic quality improvement over either pure approach.

Variants:
1. **Strict parent**: each child has one parent; return parent fully.
2. **Sliding window**: parent is a window of N sentences around the matched chunk.
3. **Chunk + summary**: child is original text, parent is a summary of the surrounding section.

LangChain's `ParentDocumentRetriever`, LlamaIndex's `RecursiveRetriever`, and most production RAG frameworks support some version. Cost is mostly indexing-time complexity and storage (you're keeping both children and parents); query-time cost is similar to standard retrieval.
why_it_matters: If you've tried both small and large chunks and neither works well — small chunks retrieve precisely but answers lack context, large chunks lose precision in the embedding — parent-document is the right architectural fix. It's one of the most consistently helpful RAG techniques and easy to drop into existing pipelines. For knowledge bases with structured documents (sections, paragraphs), parent-document is essentially the right default.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like searching a textbook. You search the index (small chunks) which points you to specific sentences. But you read the surrounding paragraph (parent) for context, not just the indexed sentence. The index is precise; the paragraph provides the flesh around the bones. Both are needed. Same with parent-document RAG: small chunks point precisely; large chunks provide reading context.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain pattern: `from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever; retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(vectorstore=vs, docstore=docstore, child_splitter=small_splitter, parent_splitter=big_splitter)`. Add documents and it indexes children, returns parents on retrieval.

related_terms_explained.connection: Parent-document retrieval is a sophisticated chunking strategy with hierarchical chunks.
Parent-document is a common production RAG architecture pattern.
Same idea by another name — retrieve small chunks, return big context.
A specific implementation where the parent is a window around the matched sentence.

code_example:
from langchain.retrievers import ParentDocumentRetriever
from langchain.storage import InMemoryStore

retriever = ParentDocumentRetriever(
    vectorstore=vectorstore,
    docstore=InMemoryStore(),
    child_splitter=child_splitter,  # small
    parent_splitter=parent_splitter, # large
)
retriever.add_documents(documents)

title: Product Quantization (PQ)
short_definition: A vector compression technique that splits each vector into sub-vectors, quantizes each sub-vector independently to a small codebook, and stores compact codes — often 50-100x compression with modest accuracy loss.
full_explanation:
Storing 1B vectors at 768 dimensions × 4 bytes = 3TB. Memory becomes the bottleneck long before compute does. Product Quantization (Jégou et al., 2010) compresses each vector dramatically. The algorithm splits a 768-dim vector into M sub-vectors (e.g., M=32 sub-vectors of 24 dimensions each). For each of the M sub-spaces, run k-means clustering on the training set to find K codewords (typically K=256 = 8 bits). Each sub-vector is then represented by the index of its nearest codeword.

After quantization, each vector is M × log₂(K) bits = 32 × 8 = 32 bytes — down from 3072 bytes. 96x compression. At query time, the query vector is also split into sub-vectors, distances to codewords are precomputed in lookup tables, and approximate distances to compressed vectors are computed by table lookups (very fast).

The accuracy cost is real but bounded — typically 1-3% recall loss for standard PQ settings. Combined with IVF (IVF-PQ in FAISS), you get the dominant approach for billion-scale vector search. Used by Spotify (audio embeddings), YouTube (video embeddings), and many image-search platforms.
why_it_matters: For practitioners running vector search at >100M scale, memory cost becomes the dominant constraint. PQ-based approaches are how Spotify, YouTube, and Pinterest index billions of items affordably. Knowing when to switch from HNSW (fast, more memory) to IVF-PQ (slower, much less memory) is a key infrastructure decision at scale. For typical RAG (under 10M vectors), HNSW remains simpler and PQ isn't worth the complexity. Above that, PQ is mandatory.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like JPEG for vectors. JPEG splits an image into 8×8 blocks and quantizes each block to a fixed set of patterns; the result is dramatically smaller with mostly-imperceptible quality loss. PQ does the same to vectors: split into chunks, replace each chunk with the nearest 'pattern' from a learned codebook, store just the pattern indices. The compression ratio (50-200x) and quality cost (1-3%) make it nearly always worth it at scale.
see_it_in_practice: FAISS IVF-PQ: `index = faiss.index_factory(768, 'IVF1024,PQ32x8'); index.train(training_vectors); index.add(all_vectors)`. The PQ32x8 spec means 32 sub-vectors with 8-bit codes each = 32 bytes per vector. Memory drops 100x; recall loses 1-3%.

related_terms_explained.connection: Product Quantization is most-commonly used as IVF-PQ — IVF clustering plus PQ compression.
FAISS pioneered production PQ implementations.
PQ is a specific application of quantization to vector representations.
PQ is the standard technique for memory-constrained ANN at billion-scale.

code_example:
import faiss
index = faiss.index_factory(768, 'IVF4096,PQ32x8')
index.train(train_vecs)
index.add(corpus_vecs)
index.nprobe = 16
D, I = index.search(query, k=10)

title: Query Rewriting
short_definition: Using an LLM to transform the user's raw query into one or more search-optimized queries — handles vague questions, ambiguous references, and conversational follow-ups.
full_explanation:
User queries are often suboptimal for retrieval. They're conversational ('what about the second one?'), incomplete ('and the price?'), or ambiguous ('how does it work?'). Query rewriting uses an LLM to transform the raw query into a search-optimized version: resolve references, expand abbreviations, add context from chat history, split compound questions.

Common strategies:
1. **Reference resolution**: 'his book' → 'Shakespeare's book' (using prior turn context).
2. **Decomposition**: 'how does HNSW compare to IVF and which uses more memory?' → 'how does HNSW work?' + 'how does IVF work?' + 'memory usage HNSW vs IVF'.
3. **Expansion**: 'GPT cost' → 'GPT-4 API pricing per token' (adding implied detail).
4. **HyDE**: generate hypothetical answer, embed that (covered separately).
5. **Step-back**: rewrite specific question into more general one to retrieve broader context, then drill in.

Production RAG agents almost always do some form of query rewriting on multi-turn conversations — without it, follow-up questions ('and what about that other thing you mentioned?') retrieve nothing useful. The cost is one extra LLM call per query; standard practice for production-grade chat-RAG.
why_it_matters: If your RAG works for first-turn queries but breaks on follow-ups, query rewriting is the missing piece. For multi-turn agent conversations and any RAG-over-chat-history use case, query rewriting is essentially mandatory. The cost (one Haiku-class call) is small; the quality improvement on conversational queries is huge. Often the difference between 'demo works' and 'shipped product works.'
how_to_think_about_it: Think of query rewriting as a translator standing between the user and the search engine. The user speaks colloquially, ambiguously, with implied context. The search engine wants explicit, complete, well-formed queries. The translator rewrites in real-time so the engine sees a clean query and the user sees a relevant answer. Without the translator, you're forcing users to phrase like they're writing Google search bars.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain's `MultiQueryRetriever` and `SelfQueryRetriever` are off-the-shelf rewrite patterns. DIY: `rewrite_prompt = f'Given this conversation: {history}\n\nRewrite the user query to be self-contained: {query}'`; `clean_query = llm.invoke(rewrite_prompt)`; use clean_query for retrieval.

related_terms_explained.connection: Query rewriting is a standard production-RAG technique for handling conversational queries.
Multi-query is a specific query rewriting strategy that produces multiple variants.
HyDE is a query rewriting strategy that produces hypothetical documents instead of new queries.
Self-query rewrites queries with structured filter extraction for vector + filter retrieval.

title: RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation)
short_definition: An architecture where you fetch relevant text from your knowledge base at query time and pass it to an LLM as context — gives the model up-to-date or proprietary information it wasn't trained on.
full_explanation:
LLMs know what they were trained on and nothing else. They can't see your company's internal docs, last week's news, or any text added since their training cutoff. RAG (Retrieval Augmented Generation) fixes this by retrieving relevant content from external sources at query time and injecting it into the prompt.

The canonical RAG pipeline:
1. **Index**: chunk documents, embed each chunk, store in a vector database.
2. **Retrieve**: at query time, embed the question, find the K most similar chunks (often combined with BM25 lexical search and reranking).
3. **Generate**: stuff the retrieved chunks into the LLM prompt with instructions to answer based on them.

Production RAG diverges from this baseline in many ways: query rewriting, hybrid search, reranking, contextual compression, parent-document retrieval, multi-query expansion, and feedback loops. The 'simple RAG' people first build typically misses 30-40% of relevant results compared to a well-tuned production setup. Most quality gains come from the surrounding plumbing, not the core LLM.

LangChain, LlamaIndex, Haystack, and Vectara provide opinionated frameworks. Building your own from scratch is a few hundred lines of code with the right embedding model, vector DB, and reranker — surprisingly approachable in 2025-2026.
why_it_matters: RAG is the dominant pattern for adding domain knowledge to LLM applications. Every customer support bot, internal Q&A system, documentation assistant, and research tool built on top of an LLM is some form of RAG. Understanding the architecture lets you debug 'why doesn't my chatbot know this?' problems, improve answer quality, and reason about cost (per-call retrieval + LLM inference). Foundational knowledge for anyone shipping LLM products.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of RAG as giving the LLM a quick research session before it answers. You don't expect the model to memorize your company's docs; instead, every query becomes a 30-second research task: search the docs, read the relevant pages, write the answer. The retrieval is the search; the generation is the answer write-up. Quality depends mostly on the retrieval — if you fetch the wrong pages, the model's answer will be wrong no matter how good the model is.
see_it_in_practice: Minimum viable RAG in 30 lines: pip install langchain chromadb sentence-transformers; load docs, chunk, embed, store in Chroma; embed query, retrieve top-5, format prompt, call LLM. From there you'll iterate on chunking, hybrid search, and reranking — each adding noticeable quality improvements.

related_terms_explained.connection: Embeddings are the foundation of vector retrieval in RAG.
Chunking is the preprocessing step where document quality is largely determined.
Vector databases store and search the chunk embeddings.
Production RAG typically uses hybrid (sparse + dense) search instead of pure vector.
Cross-encoder reranking is the most reliable post-retrieval quality improvement.

code_example:
# Minimum viable RAG
from langchain.vectorstores import Chroma
from langchain.embeddings import HuggingFaceEmbeddings
emb = HuggingFaceEmbeddings(model_name='BAAI/bge-large-en-v1.5')
vs = Chroma.from_documents(docs, emb)
docs = vs.similarity_search(query, k=5)
response = llm.invoke(f'Context: {docs}\n\nQ: {query}\nA:')

title: RAG Fusion
short_definition: Multi-query retrieval combined with reciprocal rank fusion — generate multiple query variants, retrieve for each, then merge results with RRF for better recall.
full_explanation:
RAG Fusion (Adrian Raudaschl, 2023) is a small idea with big results: take multi-query retrieval and add reciprocal rank fusion (RRF) for the merge step. The combination consistently outperforms either standalone or naive union-and-dedupe.

Workflow:
1. LLM generates 4-5 query variants from the user's question.
2. Each variant runs vector retrieval, returning ranked top-K.
3. RRF merges the K rankings into one: each candidate's final score = sum over variants of 1/(rank_in_variant + 60). The constant 60 is the standard RRF dampening.
4. Top final-ranked candidates go to the LLM.

Why this works: RRF rewards items that rank well across multiple queries (suggesting genuine relevance) over items that rank #1 in one query but absent in others (might be a coincidence). The dampening constant prevents any single ranking from dominating — even rank 1 contributes less than rank 1 + 2 across two variants.

Most production RAG frameworks support RAG Fusion as a one-line setup. Cost is one extra LLM call (variants) plus minor merge compute. Recall improvements typically 10-25% on technical or ambiguous queries.
why_it_matters: Easy production RAG upgrade with consistent quality gains. If you're already doing multi-query retrieval, switching to RAG Fusion (adding RRF) is essentially free and reliably better than naive union. For RAG over technical documentation, customer support knowledge bases, or anywhere queries can be ambiguous, RAG Fusion is a strong default.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of RAG Fusion like asking five friends to recommend restaurants and then taking the consensus list. If a restaurant shows up in three friends' top-5, it's probably good. If one friend's top pick doesn't appear on anyone else's list, it might be a personal quirk. RRF formalizes this — items that rank well across multiple perspectives get higher overall scores than items that dominate a single perspective.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain has RAG Fusion patterns; the original blog post (langchain.com/blog/rag-fusion) walks through implementation. DIY: generate variants with LLM, retrieve top-20 for each, RRF merge, take top-10.

related_terms_explained.connection: RAG Fusion is multi-query retrieval with RRF added for the merge step.
RRF is the merge algorithm that defines RAG Fusion.
RAG Fusion is a specific production-RAG enhancement pattern.
RAG Fusion is built on query-rewriting (variant generation).

title: Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF)
short_definition: A simple algorithm for merging multiple ranked lists into one — assigns each item a score of 1/(rank+k) per list, sums across lists, ranks by total. Robust and parameter-free.
full_explanation:
When you have multiple rankings of the same item set (from different retrievers, different query variants, different scoring methods), you need to merge them into one ranking. Naive averaging requires score normalization (different methods produce wildly different score scales). Reciprocal Rank Fusion (Cormack et al., 2009) sidesteps this entirely by working with ranks instead of scores.

Formula: for each item, RRF_score = sum over rankings of 1/(rank_in_ranking + k), where k=60 is the standard dampening constant. Items not present in a ranking contribute 0. Items appearing high in multiple rankings accumulate a high RRF score; items appearing once at low rank get a small score.

Why it works: rank-based scoring is scale-invariant. The dampening constant prevents any single ranking from dominating — at k=60, rank 1 contributes 1/61 ≈ 0.0164, rank 2 contributes 1/62 ≈ 0.0161, rank 100 contributes 1/160 ≈ 0.0063. The marginal benefit of being in another ranking outweighs the marginal benefit of being slightly higher in one ranking. This is what makes RRF robust.

Used in: hybrid search (BM25 + dense), multi-query retrieval (RAG Fusion), ensemble of rerankers, multi-modal retrieval (text + image rankings). Built into Elasticsearch hybrid mode, Weaviate hybrid, and most modern retrieval frameworks.
why_it_matters: RRF is the workhorse merge algorithm for any ensemble of rankings. If you're combining BM25 + vector search results and asking 'how do I merge these?', RRF is the answer. If you're running RAG Fusion or any multi-retriever ensemble, RRF is what makes it work. Five-line implementation, no hyperparameters to tune (k=60 just works), zero risk. Underrated piece of retrieval plumbing.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of RRF like Olympic medal counting. A country's medal score isn't the sum of athletes' actual point scores (which differ per sport); it's a count weighted by gold/silver/bronze (rank). Countries that consistently medal across many events outscore countries that win one event spectacularly. Same with RRF: items that rank well across many retrievers outscore items that dominate one retriever.
see_it_in_practice: Five-line Python: `def rrf(rankings, k=60): scores = {}; for r in rankings: for rank, item in enumerate(r): scores[item] = scores.get(item, 0) + 1/(rank+k); return sorted(scores, key=scores.get, reverse=True)`. That's the entire algorithm.

related_terms_explained.connection: RRF is the standard merge algorithm for hybrid sparse + dense search.
RAG Fusion is multi-query retrieval merged with RRF.
RRF is sometimes used as a lightweight reranker alternative to cross-encoders.
RRF is a foundational merge algorithm in production RAG ensembles.

title: Reranking
short_definition: Running a second, more accurate scoring model over the top candidates from initial retrieval — improves quality without scaling the more accurate model to the full corpus.
full_explanation:
Initial retrieval (vector search, BM25, hybrid) is fast but imperfect. Reranking takes the top-K (often top-50 or top-100) and reorders them with a more compute-expensive but accurate model — typically a cross-encoder that scores each (query, document) pair jointly. The final top-N (often 5-10) goes to the LLM.

Why it works: cross-encoders are 5-15% more accurate than bi-encoders at retrieval scoring but are too slow to score the full corpus. Reranking restricts cross-encoder usage to a small candidate set, getting the accuracy benefit at a manageable cost. Latency budget: typical reranker on top-50 candidates adds 200-1000ms per query.

Production options: Cohere's rerank API (paid, very strong), BAAI/bge-reranker-large (open weights, comparable quality), Jina Reranker (open weights), Voyage AI reranker (paid). All work as drop-in additions after any first-stage retriever.

Reranking interacts well with everything else: hybrid search → reranker, multi-query retrieval → reranker, parent-document retrieval → reranker. The pattern of 'broad first stage + accurate second stage' is the dominant production architecture for high-quality retrieval.
why_it_matters: If you're not reranking, you're missing the easiest single quality improvement available to RAG. Adding Cohere Rerank or BGE Reranker to existing retrieval is a 30-minute change that consistently improves end-to-end answer quality. Open weights means it's free if you self-host. The latency cost is usually acceptable; the quality gain is usually substantial. For practitioners building RAG and feeling stuck at quality, reranking is the first thing to try.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of two-stage retrieval like job hiring. Stage 1 (resume screening, fast and shallow): scan 1000 resumes for basic keyword match, pick 100. Stage 2 (interview, slow and deep): interview the 100 carefully, pick the 10 best. Doing all 1000 interviews would be impossibly slow; skipping interviews entirely and hiring from resumes alone makes terrible decisions. The two-stage funnel is what makes both quality and scale possible.
see_it_in_practice: BGE Reranker drop-in: retrieve top-50 candidates with your existing retriever, then `from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder; reranker = CrossEncoder('BAAI/bge-reranker-large'); pairs = [(query, doc) for doc in candidates]; scores = reranker.predict(pairs); top10 = [c for _, c in sorted(zip(scores, candidates), reverse=True)[:10]]`.

related_terms_explained.connection: Cross-encoders are the standard reranker architecture.
Bi-encoders are typically the first stage, rerankers are the second stage.
Reranking is one of the most common and effective production RAG enhancements.
Hybrid + rerank is a typical three-stage production pipeline.

code_example:
from sentence_transformers import CrossEncoder
reranker = CrossEncoder('BAAI/bge-reranker-large')
candidates = vectorstore.similarity_search(query, k=50)
pairs = [(query, c.page_content) for c in candidates]
scores = reranker.predict(pairs)
top10 = [c for _, c in sorted(zip(scores, candidates), reverse=True)[:10]]

title: Retrieval (IR)
short_definition: Finding documents in a collection that are relevant to a query — the foundational task that powers search engines, RAG, and recommendation systems.
full_explanation:
Information Retrieval (IR) is the broader academic discipline that retrieval inherits from. The core problem: given a query and a corpus of N documents, return the most relevant subset. 'Relevant' is the loaded word — different scoring functions optimize for different definitions.

Classical IR (1960s-2010s) was dominated by lexical methods: BM25 and TF-IDF scored by term overlap with various refinements. Modern IR added dense retrieval (semantic embeddings) and learned methods (BERT-style scorers). The current state-of-the-art is hybrid: combine lexical, dense, and learned signals, often with cross-encoder reranking.

Key metrics: recall@K (did the relevant document make it to top-K?), precision@K (how many of the top-K are relevant?), MRR (mean reciprocal rank — how high did the first relevant doc appear?), nDCG (normalized discounted cumulative gain — accounts for graded relevance and rank position). Production teams pick the metrics that matter most for their downstream task; for RAG, recall@K and nDCG@10 are typical.
