For RAG context, retrieval is the first half of the pipeline. Quality of retrieval upper-bounds quality of generation — you can't answer a question correctly if you didn't retrieve the relevant document.
why_it_matters: Retrieval is one of the oldest and most-studied problems in computer science. Decades of research went into BM25, dense retrieval, ranking metrics, and evaluation methodology. For practitioners building search-adjacent products (RAG, recommendation, semantic search, deduplication, document classification), grounding in IR fundamentals — what 'relevance' means, how to measure it, what classical methods compete with neural ones — is essential. Foundational discipline that pays off across many adjacent areas.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of retrieval as the work a librarian does. You walk in with a question; the librarian goes to the shelves and brings back books they think might help. Their judgment about 'might help' is the scoring function. Their effectiveness depends on (1) knowing the collection, (2) understanding the question, (3) matching the two well. Different librarians (algorithms) match differently — some focus on exact word matches, some on topic similarity, some combine both. The right librarian for the job depends on the questions asked.
see_it_in_practice: Get hands dirty: build a tiny retrieval system in 50 lines. Embed 1000 Wikipedia paragraphs, embed 100 test queries, measure recall@5. Then add BM25 alongside and compare. Then add a reranker. The progression teaches you what each component buys you on real text.

related_terms_explained.connection: RAG is a major modern application of retrieval techniques.
Dense retrieval is the embedding-based modern approach to retrieval.
Sparse retrieval is the lexical-matching classical approach (BM25, TF-IDF).
Embeddings power dense retrieval methods.
Reranking is the second-stage refinement common in production retrieval.

title: Self-Query Retrieval
short_definition: A retriever that uses an LLM to extract structured filters from a natural-language query, then runs vector search with those filters applied — handles 'movies from 2020 about AI' style queries.
full_explanation:
Standard vector retrieval ignores structured constraints in queries. 'Show me action movies from 2020 with rating above 8' gets embedded as one big vector that approximates 'action movies from 2020 with rating above 8' — but the date and rating constraints are now fuzzy similarities, not hard filters. Self-query retrieval fixes this by using an LLM to parse the query into (semantic intent + structured filters) before retrieval.

Workflow: LLM receives the query plus a schema of filterable metadata fields. It outputs (a) a stripped semantic query for vector search and (b) structured filters in your vector DB's filter language. The vector DB applies the structured filters and runs vector search on the semantic query.

Example: 'action movies from 2020 with rating above 8' → semantic_query='action movies', filters={'genre':'action', 'year': 2020, 'rating': {'gt': 8}}. Vector search runs on 'action movies', metadata filter pre-narrows to 2020 + rating > 8.

LangChain's `SelfQueryRetriever` and LlamaIndex's `AutoRetriever` automate this for common vector DBs (Pinecone, Qdrant, Chroma, Weaviate). Cost is one extra LLM call per query (~500ms for fast models). Quality improvement on filter-able queries is dramatic.
why_it_matters: If your data has structured metadata (dates, categories, prices, ratings, regions) and your users ask questions that mix semantic intent with constraints, self-query retrieval is essentially the right architecture. Without it, you're either ignoring constraints (bad results) or forcing users to use a structured filter UI (bad UX). With it, conversational queries that mention constraints get correctly filtered. For e-commerce, content discovery, document search with rich metadata — practically necessary.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of self-query retrieval as a translator who understands both natural language and your database's filter syntax. The user says 'cheap Italian restaurants near downtown that opened recently.' The translator converts to: semantic='Italian restaurants', filters={'price_tier': 'cheap', 'neighborhood': 'downtown', 'opened_after': '2024-01-01'}. The vector search then operates on the right candidate set with the right semantic intent.
see_it_in_practice: LangChain SelfQueryRetriever requires a metadata schema definition: `metadata_field_info=[AttributeInfo(name='year', description='movie release year', type='integer'), AttributeInfo(name='rating', description='IMDB rating 1-10', type='float')]`. Then `SelfQueryRetriever.from_llm(llm, vectorstore, document_content_description, metadata_field_info)`.

related_terms_explained.connection: Self-query retrieval generates the metadata filters that the vector DB then applies.
Self-query is a structured form of query rewriting.
Self-query is a common production-RAG pattern for filterable knowledge bases.
Self-query depends on vector DB filter capability for the structured constraint half.

title: Semantic Caching
short_definition: Caching LLM responses keyed by semantic similarity instead of exact-match query — serves cached responses to similar (not identical) queries to cut cost and latency.
full_explanation:
Standard HTTP caching matches on exact request bytes. For LLM applications, that catches almost no traffic — users phrase the same question dozens of ways. Semantic caching addresses this by embedding incoming queries, looking for past queries with high cosine similarity, and returning the cached response if found above some similarity threshold (typically 0.85-0.95).

Mechanically: incoming query → embed → vector search against cache of (query_embedding, response) pairs → if top match exceeds threshold, return cached response; otherwise call LLM and cache the new (query, response) pair.

The benefits scale with query repetition. A customer support bot sees thousands of variants of 'how do I reset my password' — semantic caching can serve 60-80% of these with cached responses, cutting LLM cost dramatically. The risks: serving wrong answers when threshold is too low (different intents that embed similarly), and stale answers when underlying knowledge has changed.

Production implementations: GPTCache (open-source, supports many backends), Redis with vector search add-on, Upstash semantic cache. Most modern LLM API gateways add semantic caching as a layer on top of basic HTTP caching.
why_it_matters: For high-traffic LLM applications, semantic caching can cut costs 50-80% with minimal quality loss when tuned correctly. For chat applications with FAQ-style traffic patterns, this is one of the highest-leverage cost optimizations available. The setup is a few hours; the savings compound with traffic scale. For low-traffic applications it's not worth the complexity; above 10K queries/day it almost always is.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of semantic caching like a customer service rep who recognizes 'I've answered this question before, even though phrased differently.' Instead of looking up the answer fresh each time, they remember and respond from memory. The trick is calibration — recognize too aggressively and you'll return wrong answers to subtly different questions; recognize too conservatively and you save nothing. The right threshold depends on how dissimilar 'similar enough' queries are in your domain.
see_it_in_practice: GPTCache: `from gptcache import cache; cache.init(); cache.set_openai_key()`. Now every OpenAI API call automatically checks the semantic cache first. Configure similarity threshold and TTL based on your domain. Monitor cache hit rate and answer quality samples to tune.

related_terms_explained.connection: Semantic caching is built on embedding-based similarity matching.
Different concept — prompt caching is provider-side; semantic caching is application-side.
Semantic caches use vector indices to find similar past queries quickly.
Semantic caching is one of the highest-impact LLM cost-reduction techniques.

title: Sentence Window Retrieval
short_definition: A RAG pattern that embeds individual sentences but returns surrounding context (a window of N sentences before and after) to the LLM — gets sentence-level precision with paragraph-level context.
full_explanation:
A specialized variant of parent-document retrieval. During indexing, each sentence is embedded individually. Each sentence vector stores a pointer to the surrounding sentence window (e.g., 3 sentences before, the matched sentence, 3 sentences after). At retrieval, sentence-level similarity finds candidates; the LLM receives the windowed context, not the bare sentence.

Why this beats standard chunking: a 512-token chunk averages many topics; the embedding flattens them all. A single sentence has one topic; its embedding is a precise signal. But the LLM still needs context around the sentence to answer well. The window provides that context without the embedding-flattening problem.

LlamaIndex was the first major framework to ship this as a first-class pattern (`SentenceWindowNodeParser`). Window sizes of 3-5 sentences each side are common; bigger windows leak into multi-topic territory; smaller windows lose context.

Best fit: Q&A systems where the answer is typically contained in a single sentence but needs surrounding sentences for comprehension. Less useful for: tasks that need long structured context (code, multi-step procedures), where standard parent-document with bigger child chunks works better.
why_it_matters: For Q&A RAG over prose-heavy corpora — Wikipedia, news articles, academic papers, customer support docs — sentence-window retrieval consistently outperforms naive chunking. The precision-context tradeoff that plagues standard chunking dissolves. For practitioners who've tried multiple chunk sizes and felt stuck between 'too precise, no context' and 'too much context, fuzzy retrieval,' sentence-window is the breakthrough.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of it like searching a transcript of an interview. You search for the specific sentence with the answer — high precision. But you read the surrounding sentences for context — to understand how the answer connects to the question and what came before. The search is sentence-level; the reading is paragraph-level. Sentence-window retrieval is just this pattern applied programmatically.
see_it_in_practice: LlamaIndex: `from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceWindowNodeParser; parser = SentenceWindowNodeParser(window_size=3); nodes = parser.get_nodes_from_documents(docs)`. Then index nodes; retrieval automatically returns windowed context.

related_terms_explained.connection: Sentence window is a specific implementation of parent-document retrieval.
An alternative chunking strategy that decouples retrieval unit from context unit.
Particularly effective for Q&A-style RAG over prose corpora.
Same pattern by another name — small for retrieval, big for context.

code_example:
from llama_index.core.node_parser import SentenceWindowNodeParser
parser = SentenceWindowNodeParser(window_size=3)
nodes = parser.get_nodes_from_documents(documents)

title: Small-to-Big Retrieval
short_definition: Retrieve based on small precise chunks but return larger context blocks containing them — same idea as parent-document retrieval, different name.
full_explanation:
A naming convention used in some frameworks (LlamaIndex notably) for the parent-document pattern. The 'small' is what you embed and search against (precise chunks for high-quality matching). The 'big' is what you return to the LLM (larger surrounding blocks for context).

Why the naming: it emphasizes the asymmetry. Naive chunking forces one size for both retrieval and context; small-to-big explicitly separates them. Small chunks for retrieval = precise matches without the embedding flattening problem of large chunks. Big chunks for context = enough surrounding information for the LLM to reason about the match.

Small units can be sentences, paragraphs, or fixed-size 256-token chunks. Big units can be entire sections, full documents, or windowed neighborhoods. The hierarchy can have multiple levels: child chunks → parent paragraphs → grandparent sections, retrieving children but returning whichever ancestor level the LLM needs.

The pattern is called parent-document retrieval in LangChain, sentence-window retrieval when implemented with sentences specifically, hierarchical retrieval in academic papers. All point at the same architectural idea.
why_it_matters: Same answer as parent-document retrieval — this is one of the most impactful RAG architectural choices. If your retrieval is hitting precision-vs-context tradeoffs, small-to-big breaks the tradeoff cleanly. For practitioners building production RAG with quality requirements, it's a near-default pattern in 2025-2026.
how_to_think_about_it: See parent-document retrieval — same mental model, different terminology. The small unit is your search index entry; the big unit is what you actually return. Think of a recipe index that points to the recipe page — you search the index (small), but you read the recipe (big). One operation, two granularities.
see_it_in_practice: LlamaIndex's `RecursiveRetriever` lets you build arbitrary small-to-big hierarchies. LangChain's `ParentDocumentRetriever` is the two-level version. Most modern RAG frameworks support some form natively.

related_terms_explained.connection: Same pattern — different name in different frameworks.
Specific small-to-big where the small is a sentence and the big is a window.
Small-to-big is a chunking strategy that uses multi-level chunks.
Standard production RAG architecture pattern.

title: Sparse Retrieval
short_definition: Retrieval based on lexical term matching (BM25, TF-IDF) — produces sparse vectors where only matched-term positions are non-zero. Strong for exact matches, weak for paraphrases.
full_explanation:
Sparse retrieval represents documents as vectors with one position per vocabulary word, with most positions zero (only matched words have non-zero values). The vector is 'sparse' because of all those zeros. Sparse retrievers compute similarity by counting matched terms with various weighting schemes (TF-IDF, BM25).

The classical sparse retrievers — BM25 and TF-IDF — predate neural NLP. They remain competitive baselines because they handle exact term matches, rare words, and proper nouns better than dense retrievers. Modern variants like SPLADE add learned weights to sparse vectors, getting some of the semantic benefits of dense methods while keeping sparse retrieval's strengths.

Production stack: Elasticsearch, OpenSearch, Tantivy, Lucene all implement BM25 as their default scorer. The infrastructure is mature, fast, and resource-efficient (sparse indexes are smaller than dense vectors per document). Most modern hybrid search setups combine sparse retrieval with dense retrieval for best results.

Why sparse persists in 2026: it captures something dense retrieval can't — the pure signal of word overlap. When a user searches for 'GPT-4' or 'Anthropic' or any specific term, sparse retrieval handles it cleanly. Dense retrieval can drift to semantically similar but lexically different content, which is sometimes useful and sometimes catastrophic.
why_it_matters: If you're building search and skipping sparse retrieval, you're missing handling of named entities, rare terms, technical vocabulary, and exact-phrase queries — categories where dense retrieval is unreliable. Hybrid search (sparse + dense) is the modern best practice. For practitioners building RAG, customer search, or any retrieval-driven product, sparse retrieval is a foundational competence.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of sparse retrieval like the find-in-page feature of your browser. It looks for exact words. Useful when you know what word you're looking for. Useless when the document phrases the same idea differently. Dense retrieval is the opposite — finds similar topics but might miss your specific keyword. The two complement perfectly: keyword search for what you specifically said, semantic search for what you might mean.
see_it_in_practice: Elasticsearch comes with BM25 default; for Python: `pip install rank_bm25; bm25 = BM25Okapi([doc.split() for doc in corpus]); scores = bm25.get_scores(query.split())`. SPLADE for learned sparse: `pip install pyserini` and use the SPLADE encoder.

related_terms_explained.connection: BM25 is the dominant sparse retrieval scoring function.
Dense retrieval is the semantic counterpart; both are typically combined in hybrid search.
SPLADE is the modern 'learned sparse' approach combining sparse retrieval with neural networks.
Hybrid combines sparse and dense retrieval for best-of-both-worlds performance.
TF-IDF is the classical sparse retrieval scoring; BM25 improves on it.

title: SPLADE (Sparse Learned Retrieval)
short_definition: A 'learned sparse' retriever that uses a BERT model to produce sparse vectors with weights over a 30K-token vocabulary — combines sparse retrieval's strengths with neural learned representations.
full_explanation:
SPLADE (SParse Lexical AnD Expansion model, Formal et al., 2021) bridges sparse and dense retrieval. Standard sparse retrieval uses fixed term weights (TF-IDF or BM25). Standard dense retrieval uses learned dense embeddings. SPLADE uses learned sparse embeddings: a BERT model takes a query or document and outputs a vector over the entire BERT vocabulary (~30K positions) where each position has a learned non-negative weight.

The vectors are sparse (most positions are zero — the model learns which words matter). They're learned (the model can put weight on words not literally in the input — query expansion). Inverted-index infrastructure (BM25-style) can serve them directly with minimal modification. The result: better than BM25 on standard benchmarks, often competitive with dense retrievers, with sparse-retrieval's interpretability and infrastructure compatibility.

SPLADEv2 and SPLADE++ refined the architecture. Pyserini and Vespa support SPLADE in production. Practical adoption is growing in 2024-2026 as teams realize hybrid + reranker isn't always needed if you can get sparse-retrieval-quality from a single neural model. Inference cost is similar to dense embedding (one BERT forward pass per query); index serving cost is lower than dense (sparse vectors compress better).
why_it_matters: For practitioners optimizing retrieval who've already done hybrid + reranking and want to push further, SPLADE is one of the more interesting research-to-production directions. For greenfield retrieval systems, SPLADE alone often matches hybrid + reranker performance with simpler infrastructure. Worth knowing about for anyone serious about retrieval, even if mainstream adoption is still catching up.
how_to_think_about_it: Think of standard BM25 as a librarian using a fixed dictionary to score relevance. Dense retrievers as a librarian who's read so many books they have intuition about meaning. SPLADE is a librarian using an enriched dictionary that includes synonyms and related terms learned from reading: 'this query is about cars; my dictionary tells me to also weight automobile, vehicle, truck, sedan.' Same infrastructure as classical sparse, but the weights are smarter.
see_it_in_practice: Pyserini ships SPLADE encoders: `from pyserini.encode import SpladeQueryEncoder; encoder = SpladeQueryEncoder('naver/splade-cocondenser-ensembledistil')`. Or via huggingface for direct use. Production deployment is similar to BM25 — it serves through inverted indexes with vector dot products.

related_terms_explained.connection: SPLADE is a learned sparse retrieval — neural-network-trained version of BM25-style methods.
SPLADE outperforms BM25 by learning term weights instead of using fixed formulas.
SPLADE often matches dense retrieval quality with sparse retrieval infrastructure.
Both are middle-ground approaches improving on standard bi-encoder retrieval.
